flags.message: No snapshot or record directory was provisioned. Predicting purely from base rates as instructed.

The brief in opposition (docketed Jul 14 2026, three days before provisioning) was not provisioned under record/documents/ — documents.json lists only the petition and questions-presented. Forward mode allowed me to fetch and extract the BIO PDF from supremecourt.gov myself, but predictors on stricter cells would have missed the respondent's vehicle arguments. Separately, fedcourts query returned zero rows for every topic tried, so corpus priors contributed nothing to this cell.
fedcourts query corpus-backend ranged failed because FEDCOURTS_CORPUS_SERVICE_URL or corpus_remote_url is not set in this environment.

Decisive forward signal from a companion case (not leakage): the provisioned union BIO reports certiorari was denied in Macy's, Inc. v. NLRB, No. 25-627, on June 15, 2026 — a cleaner vehicle for this petition's strongest question (the Thryv consequential-damages split). That denial weighed heavily in my denial forecast here.

Tooling drift, not a case problem: the predict prompt tells predictors to anchor on the statpack's 'Segment base rate by salience band' table, but the committed metrics/statpack.md contains no such section; and fedcourts query returned zero rows for every topic tried (including single-word topics like 'tribe'), so no similar-prior retrieval was possible. Prediction anchors on the modern per-Term cert base rates plus the docket's call-for-response signal instead.

Decisive forward signal from a companion case: the Court granted certiorari in the lead companion petition, RNC v. Mi Familia Vota (No. 25-1017), on 2026-06-29 (corpus row + ballot-access.org), while leaving this petition (25-1022) and 25-1019 unacted-on — i.e., held. The SG's consolidated brief recommended granting 25-1017 and denying this petition. This companion-grant/hold pattern, not the provisioned docket alone, drives the prediction (P(grant incl. GVR)=0.20, predicted denial). Not leakage: it is pre-decision public information about a different petition; this case's own disposition does not yet exist.

Decisive forward signal from a companion case, disclosed per the retrieval contract: the Court granted certiorari in the vided companion RNC v. Mi Familia Vota (25-1017) around 2026-06-29/07-02 (predates the provisioned 2026-07-17 snapshot), while this petition (25-1019) shows no action after the 6/25 conference — i.e., it is being held. This petition's own disposition remains undecided; the prediction treats it as a held same-judgment companion (modal outcome denial, material GVR probability).

The provisioned snapshot and documents are missing in the data directory. Provisioning likely refused this forward cell because the snapshot's latest entry reads terminal (case already decided). Predicting from priors and base rates only per the instructions.
The `fedcourts query` tool with --corpus-backend ranged failed because the corpus remote URL was not present in the environment.

The brief in opposition (filed Jul 15 2026, link present in the 2026-07-16 snapshot) was not provisioned under record/documents/ — documents.json lists only the petition and questions-presented. I fetched the BIO directly from the supremecourt.gov docket link (forward mode) and it materially changed the forecast: it discloses an unopposed, already-granted Third Amended Complaint in the district court that pleads the previously omitted facts, a significant vehicle defect. If the document fetcher runs before BIO text extraction, cells one day after a BIO lands may systematically miss the single most predictive document.
Attempted to use `fedcourts query` with `--corpus-backend ranged` but it failed because `corpus_remote_url` was missing from the environment.

The committed statpack in this cell contains no salience-band section or per-Term salience segment rates, although the prediction prompt directs predictors to use them. The forecast instead used paid-petition, originating-circuit, relist, and CVSG base rates.

The cell supplied the event definition but no record/context.json, snapshot, documents.json, or filed-document text. Following the missing-forward-snapshot rule, the prediction uses only committed aggregate base rates and does not retrieve the case's current docket or outcome.

Cell provisioned as 'forward' (snapshot dates to 2026-07-16), but web retrieval surfaced that the Supreme Court already granted certiorari on June 29, 2026. Treating cell as mis-provisioned and predicting from pre-decision record only per prompt instructions.
No provisioned snapshot or record found. Predicting from title and base rates only.

The provisioned record/documents/brief-in-opposition.txt is Northampton County's 9-page BIO (docket entry Jun 01 2026, '25-962 25-967 BIO.pdf'), not the principal brief in opposition of the lead respondents Bette Eakin, DSCC, DCCC, and AFT Pennsylvania, which was filed the same day and is the substantively central opposition. The prediction folds in the Northampton BIO and infers the lead BIO's existence only from the docket. Document-fetch heuristics preferring the lead respondent's BIO would strengthen the guaranteed-common input for multi-BIO cert cells.

Disposition timing note for evaluation scheduling: the Court issued a CVSG on Jun 29 2026, so this petition will likely not reach disposition until the Solicitor General files (realistically winter 2026 - spring 2027), and a hold pending Baxter v. Philadelphia Board of Elections (Pa. Supreme Court, argued Sep 10 2025, undecided as of the snapshot) could extend that further. A Baxter ruling striking the date requirement on state grounds would likely convert the outcome to a Munsingwear GVR.

Decisive forward signal from a companion case (pre-snapshot public information, not leakage): cert was granted in the lead case Nielsen v. Watanabe, No. 25-417, on 2026-06-22, and this petition's docket shows the classic hold pattern (distributed 6/11 and 6/18/2026, no order since). The prediction (P=0.85 grant, likeliest disposition GVR) is largely derivative of Nielsen's expected outcome, so this cell's realized disposition will not resolve until Nielsen is decided (~June 2027).

The provisioned questions-presented.txt is a table-of-contents fragment rather than the questions presented, and documents.json marks the petition extraction truncated. Both questions and the full cert-stage argument were intact in petition.txt and the complete brief in opposition, so the prediction used those sources.

Provisioned record/documents/questions-presented.txt captured a table-of-contents fragment rather than the petition's Questions Presented page (the QP-section extractor appears to have matched the TOC's 'are exceptionally important' lines). Not blocking: petition.txt contains the full QP page and was used instead. Flagging so the QP extractor can be checked against this docket's petition PDF.

Forward-mode disclosure: decisive public signals predating the snapshot were used — Louisiana v. Callais (decided 4/29/2026), the Court's May 2026 wave of GVRs in light of Callais, and the State of Washington's 6/2/2026 response brief asking for a GVR. No information about this petition's own (nonexistent) disposition was sought or found.

gaps.label: The corpus-prior remote was unreachable because DNS resolution failed.

A 'response requested (CFR)' cut in the statpack, analogous to the relist and CVSG cuts — the CFR is the dominant observable signal for this cell and I had to estimate its lift from general knowledge.
A BIO was not yet filed, so nothing to weigh against the petition — expected for a pending case, not a tooling defect.
A CFR (response requested) cut in the statpack would help; it is the single biggest signal on this docket and I had to price it from general knowledge
A capital-case or case-type cut in the statpack (state-PCR capital petitions grant at a very different rate than the paid aggregate) would sharpen the anchor
A conditional base rate P(grant | CVSG + SG recommendation) would sharpen CVSG cells; the corpus CVSG bucket pools both recommendation outcomes
A conditional base rate P(grant | CVSG, by SG recommendation) would sharpen post-CVSG cells like this one
A conditional base rate for call-for-response petitions would be the single most useful cut for waived-then-CFR cells like this one
A cut for 'published dissent from denial of rehearing en banc below' would be valuable — it is a known cert signal with no corpus surface.
A direct tool to parse or query PDF/text filings semantically would reduce the reliance on grep.

helpful.label: Provisioned petition and brief in opposition
Complete provisioned petition and brief in opposition
CVSG and fee-class base rates in the committed statpack
Provisioned petition and complete brief in opposition
Provisioned petition, questions presented, and brief in opposition

gaps: Could not locate the 'Segment base rate by salience band' table mentioned in the prompt within statpack.md or statpack.json.
helpful: mcp_courtlistener_search was excellent for confirming the circuit split in lieu of full-text corpus search.
recent.notes: Relied on MCP CourtListener search to verify the existence and depth of the circuit split as the corpus query tool does not support full-text search.

gaps: A 'response requested (CFR)' cut in the statpack, analogous to the relist and CVSG cuts — the CFR is the dominant observable signal for this cell and I had to estimate its lift from general knowledge.
The prompt-referenced 'Segment base rate by salience band' table is absent from the committed statpack build.

fedcourts query --citation returned 0 rows for both a statute and case citations at scotus scope; cert-stage dockets likely lack opinion-text citation indexing, making the priors surface near-useless for cert cells.
A BIO was not yet filed, so nothing to weigh against the petition — expected for a pending case, not a tooling defect.
helpful: The provisioned full petition text was the single most valuable input — the split map, vehicle claims, and respondent's concession all came from it.
The per-Term paid/IFP fee-class grant rates in statpack.json gave a much better anchor (paid ~5.4%) than the blended ~2.5% rate.
recent.notes: Forward cell in clean shape: fresh snapshot (through Jul 15, 2026), full petition text, no leakage encountered.

gaps: Free-text corpus query for priors would be more direct than relying entirely on MCP for text searches.
helpful: The MCP tool was useful to quickly verify the absence of recent SCOTUS opinions cleanly resolving the 'continuous sexual abuse' unanimity question.
recent.notes: Cell ran successfully in forward mode.

gaps: statpack has no call-for-response (CFR) cut — the dominant docket signal here had to be weighed from general knowledge rather than corpus data
the 'Segment base rate by salience band' table the predict prompt points to is absent from the committed statpack version
fedcourts query --citation matched nothing (citations appear unpopulated for modern cert dockets), so topical prior retrieval from the corpus was effectively unavailable
no way to retrieve outcomes of prior SCOTUS petitions raising the same QP (issue-level prior petitions) — CourtListener SCOTUS docket full-text search returned nothing
helpful: statpack.json per-fee-class Term detail (paid ~5.4% vs IFP ~1.1%) — the single most useful base-rate cut for this cell
provisioned petition.txt including the full lower-court opinion in the appendix — enough to assess the vehicle without any PDF fetching
CourtListener opinion search to confirm no SCOTUS merits precedent on the question
recent.notes: Forward cell, clean inputs. The BIO does not exist yet (extended to 2026-07-24), so the prediction rests on the petition side plus docket signals; a CFR-conditioned base rate in the statpack would have tightened calibration materially.

gaps: Text search over corpus priors would be helpful (fedcourts query lacks a --text option, requiring fallback to MCP search which may not cover lower court priors as effectively without exact citations).
helpful: statpack.md provided a very clear baseline for cert grant rates. Reading the BIO through bash tools allowed for quick evaluation of vehicle problems.
recent.notes: Used MCP search to pull up the main precedents cited (Williams-Yulee, White).

gaps: The statpack has no call-for-response (CFR) cut, which was the single strongest live signal on this docket (respondent waived, Court requested a response); the CFR grant-rate multiplier had to come from general knowledge rather than corpus data.
The 'Segment base rate by salience band' table the prompt points to is not present in the committed statpack.md, so the recommended band anchor was unavailable.

Three fedcourts query calls (citation- and topic-filtered, era 2020s) returned zero rows despite the corpus holding ~2,200 live/historical cases — either the citation index does not cover cert-stage papers or the filters are too exact to be useful for issue-similarity retrieval.
helpful: The provisioned petition and brief-in-opposition texts were excellent — the BIO in particular carried the vehicle-defect analysis that drove the prediction; no CourtListener retrieval was needed at all.
The statpack's relist/CVSG signal cuts and per-Term grant rates gave concrete anchors for the CFR adjustment.
recent.notes: Forward-mode cell, cleanly provisioned; snapshot fetched the same day as the run and included the BIO filed four days earlier.

gaps: fedcourts query returned no rows for citation- or topic-filtered scotus/2020s lookups (three attempts), so no comparable priors could be retrieved; a 'petitions where the Court requested a response after waiver' signal cut in the statpack would have been the most on-point base rate for this cell
The reply brief (filed Jul 14, 2026) was on the docket but not provisioned; it presumably contests the waiver finding and would have sharpened the vehicle-defect weighing
This statpack build lacks the salience-band table the prompt points to as the primary anchor
helpful: Provisioned petition and BIO full text — the BIO's waiver/vehicle argument (with the trial-court orders in its appendix) was the decisive input and would have been invisible from the docket snapshot alone
Statpack modern-cert and CVSG/relist cuts as quantitative anchors
recent.notes: Forward-mode cell; retrieval stayed within the corpus tooling — the provisioned record was rich enough that live CourtListener retrieval added no expected value before the 9/28/2026 conference.
